question: Integrated training
answer: train with optimal posture in all planes of motion to develop high levels of dynamic flexibility, core, strength, neuromuscular control, power, speed/agility/quickness, and functional strength

question: Functional continuum
answer: all three planes of motion, multidimensional movement, entire muscle-contraction spectrum, entire contraction-velocity spectrum, all acute variables manipulated

question: Neuromuscular efficiency
answer: ability of neuromuscular system to produce and reduce forces, and stabilize the entire HMS Stabilization strength, ability of stabilizing muscles to provide dynamic stabilization and postural equilibrium during functional activities

question: Force-couple
answer: muscles working together to produce movement around a joint (agonists, synergists, antagonists)

question: Integrated sports performance program
answer: flexibility, metabolic energy system, core, balance, plyometric, speed/agility/quickness, resistance, and sport-specific training

question: Exercise selection criteria
answer: safe, challenging, progressive, systematic, proprioceptively enriched, activity-specific

question: Exercise selection variables
answer: plane of motion, body position, base of support, lower/upper extremity symmetry, external resistance, balance modality

question: Exercise progression continuum
answer: progress exercises from: slow to fast, known to unknown, stable to unstable, controlled to dynamic functional movement, low to high force, correct execution to increased intensity

question: Phase 1: Stabilization Endurance
answer: increase stabilization strength, develop optimal nervous-muscular system communication; proprioceptive progressions; correct muscle imbalances; increase flexibility, joint and postural stabilization

question: Phase 2: Strength Endurance
answer: continue enhancing stabilization strength and endurance while increasing prime mover strength

question: Phase 3: Hypertrophy
answer: maximize muscle growth

question: Phase 4: Maximal Strength
answer: maximize prime mover strength

question: Phase 5: Power
answer: enhance prime mover strength while improving rate of force production

question: Phase 6: Maximal Power
answer: produce maximal acceleration and rate of force production; reserved for high-level athletes

question: Lever I
answer: Teeter-totter Flexion-extension of the head

question: Lever II
answer: Wheelbarrow Dorsiflexion-rising up on tiptoes

question: Lever III
answer: Lifting a shovel Forearm flexion

question: Length-tension relationship
answer: resting length of a muscle and the tension it can produce at that length

question: Force-velocity curve
answer: muscles’ ability to produce tension at differing shortening velocities

question: Rate of force production
answer: how quickly a muscle can generate force

question: Behavioral properties of muscle
answer: extensibility, elasticity, irritability, ability to develop tension

question: Local muscular system
answer: stabilization system; muscles connect directly to spine; transverse abdominis, multifidus, internal obliques, diaphragm, pelvic floor muscles

question: Peripheral joint support system
answer: appendicular skeletal muscle; poor force production; isolated stabilization function; rotator cuff, gluteus medius (posterior fibers), vastus medialis oblique

question: Global muscular system
answer: movement musculature; four distinct subsystems

question: Deep longitudinal subsystem (DLS)
answer: transmits force between trunk and ground; erector spinae, thoracolumbar fascia, sacrotuberous ligament, biceps femoris, peroneus longus

question: Posterior oblique subsystem (POS)
answer: transfers force from transverse to sagittal plane; works synergistically with DLS; important for rotational activities; latissimus dorsi and contralateral gluteus maximus, cross-body bridge through thoracolumbar fascia

question: Anterior oblique subsystem (AOS)
answer: similar to POS on front side of body; internal and external obliques, adductor complex, hip external rotators

question: Lateral subsystem
answer: frontal plane and pelvo-femoral stability; gluteus medius, tensor fascia latae, adductor complex, quadratus lumborum

question: Proprioception
answer: cumulative neural input from sensory afferents to the CNS

question: Internal feedback
answer: information provided by length-tension relationships, force-couple relationships, and arthrokinematics to monitor movement and environment

question: External feedback
answer: information provided by an external source

question: Knowledge of results
answer: informs athlete about the outcome of performance

question: Knowledge of performance
answer: informs athlete about the quality of movement during exercise

question: Sensorimotor integration
answer: ability of CNS to gather and interpret information to execute the proper motor response

question: Motor behavior
answer: motor response to internal and external stimuli

question: Motor control
answer: how the CNS integrates sensory information with previous experience to produce a motor response

question: Motor learning
answer: integration of motor control with practice and experience; creates relatively permanent change in ability to produce skilled movement

question: Motor development
answer: change in motor behavior over a lifetime

question: Subjective information—personal information given by the athlete; medical history and lifestyle
Readiness for activity—collect medical history
answer: such as past injuries

question: conditions before training a new athlete
Past injuries—prior injuries can have effects up and down the kinetic chain; most importantly ankle sprains
answer: and injuries

question: to knee ligaments
answer: the low back

question: Objective information—measureable data obtained by performing assessments and observations
Physiological assessments—resting heart rate
answer: blood pressure

question: Resting heart rate—70 BPM for average adult
answer: can range from 60 to 100 BPM
